one_line_takeaway: Purely natural-language edits to a Skill's SKILL.md manifest -- with no code changes -- can manipulate which AI-agent skills are discovered (up to 86% win rate, 80% Top-10), which are selected by agents (77.6% average preference for adversarial variants), and which pass registry security vetting (context-window-overflow evasion drives the malicious-block rate to 0%), exposing a registry-facing semantic supply-chain risk across the Agent Skill lifecycle.
key_finding: Discovery: black-box beam-search against OpenAI/text-embedding-3-small achieves an 86.14% pairwise win rate with a 16.40% average relative score boost (same-model, diagonal case), and reaches 80.00% Top-10 placement; triggers optimized on BAAI models transfer to OpenAI at roughly 79-80% win rate, while OpenAI-optimized triggers transfer less effectively back to BAAI models (asymmetric transfer). Under the realistic ClawHub-style popularity-aware ranking score, modified skills still win in 74.14% of 'average-day' cases; in the stricter 0-day cold-start scenario, adversarial variants win 94.00% of cases at launch, dropping to 40.00% after one hour of accumulated downloads. Selection: description-only edits shift agent choice to the adversarial variant in 77.6% of paired trials on average across 4 models and 4 strategies (average dominance ratio ~3.47x); False Advertising and Assertive Cues are the strongest strategies (up to 94.7%/5.3% for gemma-4-31B under Assertive Cues per Figure 4), and gemma-4-31B and qwen-3-235B exceed 80% average adversarial-selection rate, with every tested model selecting above the 50% chance baseline. Governance: baseline direct malicious-instruction insertion is caught effectively (68.2% labeled malicious, 0% clean, so ~31.8% of variants -- suspicious-labeled -- would remain accessible under a policy that blocks only 'malicious' verdicts); LLM Judge Jailbreaking gives only limited evasion improvement (63.5% still malicious); Paraphrasing is substantially more effective, causing 73.9% of variants to avoid a malicious verdict; Definition-of-Done framing yields 23.9% clean and 66.3% non-blocked; Context-Window Overflow is the strongest attack, with 87.1% of variants labeled clean and 0% labeled malicious, so all variants bypass blocking.
core_contribution: The paper formulates SKILL.md (the natural-language manifest/instruction file of AI-agent 'Skills' packages) as a semantic supply-chain attack surface and empirically demonstrates SKILL.md-only attacks across three registry-facing lifecycle stages -- Discovery, Selection, and Governance -- using a shared corpus of 100 real ClawHub.ai skills (20 each from email, travel, tax, health, prompt categories). All attacks modify only the natural-language text of SKILL.md, leaving executable code and auxiliary files untouched. (1) Discovery: beam-search (black-box, using Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct as proposal model, budget T=20 tokens, beam B=4, k=50) and gradient-based (white-box, 20-token trigger, 5 restarts, top-k=5, 50 iterations) trigger-optimization attacks against embedding retrievers (BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5, BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5, OpenAI/text-embedding-3-small), plus a ClawHub-style realistic ranking function combining lexical relevance, vector relevance, and download-based popularity. (2) Selection: a paired-choice experiment where only the SKILL.md description field is modified with one of four framing strategies (False Advertising, Assertive Cues, Active Maintenance, Trust/Security), tested with the Hermes-agent framework across GPT-4.1-mini, GPT-5, Gemma-4-31B, and Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct. (3) Governance: a locally-built three-stage vetting pipeline (rule-based static scanner + LLM reviewer 'ClawScan' + VirusTotal scan) modeled on ClawHub's scanning system, tested against four evasion strategies for malicious instructions inserted into 47 originally-clean skills.
threat_model: Black-box supply-chain adversary who can submit a new skill or modify an attacker-controlled skill in a registry, controlling only the textual SKILL.md content (name, description, metadata, instructions) -- not the registry backend, other users' benign skills, the base agent model, or the query distribution. The adversary can observe registry outputs (rankings, scanner verdicts, agent behavior) but does not know exact retriever/selection-policy/governance-pipeline parameters; the paper additionally evaluates a stronger white-box/surrogate-model variant for the discovery-manipulation attack where the embedding model's gradients are accessible. Three formalized attacker objectives: (a) Discovery -- raise a target skill's relevance score/rank for a target query; (b) Selection -- bias an agent's choice toward the adversarial variant over a functionally equivalent original; (c) Governance bypass -- preserve malicious intent in SKILL.md while causing the vetting pipeline to avoid a blocking verdict.
limitations: Explicitly scoped to SKILL.md-only attacks; attacks on auxiliary files, executable code, dependencies, or runtime behavior are out of scope. The 100-skill corpus spans 5 categories from a single registry (ClawHub) and may not generalize to other registries, domains, or skill formats. The governance pipeline is ClawHub-inspired but independently, locally implemented (not the actual production ClawHub scanner), validated only against ClawHub's public moderation reports, and could diverge from real production defenses as they evolve. Selection experiments use a paired-choice setup with only 4 models and a fixed set of manipulation strategies; real-world attacker techniques, other agent selection policies, and other underlying models could behave differently. The malicious instructions used for governance evasion were synthetically generated (by GPT-5) and manually reviewed rather than drawn from real in-the-wild malicious skills.

datasets_used: 100 real ClawHub.ai skills (20 each from email, travel, tax, health, prompt categories), collected by top-download ranking per category
3,000 randomly sampled ClawHub skills used to estimate average download count (579) and build the download-count distribution (Figure 6)
50 newest ClawHub skills tracked for downloads over 1 hour for the 0-day attack scenario
47 'purely clean' skills (subset of the 100-skill corpus that pass all three governance scans and are in English) used as the base corpus for governance-evasion experiments
94 synthetic malicious skill variants generated via GPT-5 (2 per clean skill, manually reviewed) for governance baseline/evasion testing
400 adversarial selection-stage variants (100 skills x 4 manipulation strategies)
quantitative_results: Discovery beam-search black-box, same-model diagonal: 86.14% win rate / +16.40% avg score boost on OpenAI/text-embedding-3-small; 53.47%/+1.22% on bge-base; 44.55%/+1.03% on bge-small
Discovery white-box gradient attack, same-model diagonal: 84.76%/+3.62% (OpenAI), 63.81%/+3.02% (bge-base), 56.19%/+4.23% (bge-small)
Top-k placement under OpenAI retrieval (black-box direct attack): Top-3 56.00%, Top-5 65.00%, Top-10 80.00%
ClawHub-style realistic ranking: average-day attack win rate 74.14%; 0-day attack win rate 94.00% at launch (0-hour), falling to 40.00% after 1 hour

Selection: average adversarial selection rate 77.6% across 4 models x 4 strategies (average dominance ratio 3.47x); per-model averages: gpt-4.1-mini 68.2%, gpt-5 71.4%, gemma-4-31B 84.3%, qwen-3-235B 79.5%; strongest single cell reported gemma-4-31B under Assertive Cues at 94.7%/5.3% (17.76x dominance ratio)
Governance baseline (direct malicious insertion, no evasion): 0% clean, 68.2% malicious, 31.8% suspicious (i.e., ~31.8% not blocked under a malicious-only blocking policy)
LLM Judge Jailbreaking evasion: 63.5% still malicious (limited improvement over baseline)
Paraphrasing evasion: 73.9% of variants avoid a malicious verdict
Definition-of-Done evasion: 23.9% labeled clean, 66.3% avoid a blocking (malicious) verdict
Context-Window Overflow evasion: 87.1% labeled clean, 0% labeled malicious -- 100% of variants avoid blocking
baselines_compared: Original (unmodified) SKILL.md retrieval scores/ranks as the discovery baseline
Direct malicious-instruction insertion (no evasion strategy) as the governance baseline
Random 50% selection as the chance baseline for the selection-manipulation experiment
Cross-model transfer comparisons (BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5, BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5, OpenAI/text-embedding-3-small) used as baselines/controls for each other in the discovery transferability analysis
